name: Evanston
vibe: Northwestern University's home, with a dense, walkable downtown and lakefront parks.
description: Evanston is the North Shore's most urban suburb, combining Northwestern's campus, a Metra and CTA-connected downtown, and its own lakefront beaches, making it a common landing spot for people who want suburban schools without giving up walkability.
commute: CTA Purple Line + Metra Union Pacific North, roughly 25-35 minutes to the Loop
schools: Evanston Township High School (District 202); highly rated District 65 elementary schools
photoCredit: Downtown Evanston skyline, photo by Nutmegger, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Home to Northwestern University's main campus.
Evanston was the birthplace of the Woman's Christian Temperance Union in 1874 and remained a dry town — no alcohol sales — until 1972.
distanceFromLoop: About 12 miles north of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 30-35 minutes via the Purple Line or Metra

name: Wilmette
vibe: Classic North Shore suburb with the Baha'i Temple, top-rated schools, and lakefront beaches.
description: Wilmette is a quintessential North Shore family suburb, known for New Trier High School's reputation, tree-lined streets, and Gillson Park's beach and harbor.
commute: Metra Union Pacific North, roughly 30-40 minutes to the Loop
schools: New Trier High School; District 39 elementary
photoCredit: Bahá'í House of Worship, Wilmette, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: James L. Kraft — founder of Kraft Foods
funFacts: Home to the Bahá'í House of Worship for North America — the only Bahá'í temple on the continent, drawing about 250,000 visitors a year and named one of the "Seven Wonders of Illinois."
The village is named for Antoine Ouilmette, a French-Canadian fur trader who was awarded 1,280 acres of land here and in Evanston in 1829.
distanceFromLoop: About 14 miles north of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 35-40 minutes via Metra Union Pacific North

name: Winnetka / Kenilworth
vibe: Among the wealthiest suburbs in the country, with large lots and elite public schools.
description: Winnetka and neighboring Kenilworth represent the top of the North Shore market, with large historic homes, extensive lakefront parks, and New Trier High School consistently ranked among the top public schools nationally.
commute: Metra Union Pacific North, roughly 35-45 minutes to the Loop
schools: New Trier High School
famousResidents: Ann-Margret — actress, New Trier High School alumna
Ivan Albright — painter and printmaker, New Trier High School alumnus
funFacts: The house used as the McCallister family home in Home Alone (1990) is a real private residence on Lincoln Avenue.
Winnetka's median household income exceeded $250,000 in 2023.
Martin Luther King Jr. spoke on Winnetka's Village Green in 1965 as part of the North Shore Summer Project; a plaque there commemorates the visit.
rankings: 2nd-richest place in Illinois — Bloomberg's Richest Places Annual Index, 2019
distanceFromLoop: About 16 miles north of the Loop — roughly 30-35 minutes by car, 35-45 minutes via Metra Union Pacific North
photoCredit: 671 Lincoln Avenue, the real Home Alone house, via Wikimedia Commons

name: Highland Park
vibe: Ravine-cut streets, Ravinia Festival's outdoor concerts, and a walkable downtown.
description: Highland Park is known for the Ravinia Festival (the Chicago Symphony Orchestra's summer home), a mix of architecture from Prairie-style to contemporary, and a downtown with its own Metra stop.
commute: Metra Union Pacific North, roughly 45-55 minutes to the Loop
schools: Highland Park High School; District 112 elementary
photoCredit: Ravinia, Highland Park, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Michael Jordan, Scottie Pippen, and Toni Kukoc — lived in Highland Park during the Bulls' 1990s championship era
funFacts: Ravinia Festival, an outdoor music venue in Highland Park, has been the summer home of the Chicago Symphony Orchestra since 1936 and is the oldest outdoor music festival in North America.
The National Register of Historic Places lists several Frank Lloyd Wright-designed homes in Highland Park, including the Willits House.
distanceFromLoop: About 25 miles north of the Loop — roughly 40-45 minutes by car, 55-60 minutes via Metra Union Pacific North

name: Oak Park
vibe: The largest concentration of Frank Lloyd Wright buildings anywhere, and the closest walkable suburb to the city.
description: Oak Park borders the city directly and offers CTA train service into the Loop, making it feel like an extension of the city while adding larger lots, its own school district, and the country's densest collection of Frank Lloyd Wright and Prairie School architecture.
commute: CTA Green Line + Metra Union Pacific West, roughly 20-30 minutes to the Loop
schools: Oak Park and River Forest High School; District 97 elementary
photoCredit: Frank Lloyd Wright Home and Studio, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Ernest Hemingway — born and raised in Oak Park
Frank Lloyd Wright — lived and worked in Oak Park for 20 years, designing many of his early Prairie School buildings there
funFacts: Home to the largest concentration of Frank Lloyd Wright-designed buildings anywhere in the world.
distanceFromLoop: About 9 miles west of the Loop — roughly 20-25 minutes by car, 25-30 minutes via the Green Line

name: River Forest
vibe: Oak Park's quieter, more residential neighbor along the Des Plaines River.
description: River Forest shares Oak Park's architectural pedigree and high school district but trades density for larger, quieter residential lots along the river.
commute: Metra Union Pacific West, roughly 25-30 minutes to the Loop
schools: Oak Park and River Forest High School
photoCredit: Chauncey Williams House, River Forest, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Home to two universities, Dominican University and Concordia University Chicago.
The Winslow House, Frank Lloyd Wright's first independent commission, was built in River Forest in 1894.
distanceFromLoop: About 10 miles west of the Loop — roughly 20-25 minutes by car, 30 minutes via Metra Union Pacific West

name: La Grange
vibe: A compact, walkable downtown with two Metra lines and a strong small-town feel.
description: La Grange punches above its size with a genuinely walkable downtown of shops and restaurants, served by two separate Metra lines, making it popular with commuting families who still want a main-street feel.
commute: Metra BNSF or Metra Heritage Corridor, roughly 25-30 minutes to the Loop
schools: Lyons Township High School; District 102 / 105 elementary
photoCredit: La Grange Village Hall, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Sarah Wayne Callies — actress (Prison Break, The Walking Dead)
funFacts: The La Grange Pet Parade has run every year since 1947 and draws thousands of spectators and their pets through downtown.

Frank Lloyd Wright designed a "bootleg house" in La Grange — one of several secret commissions he took on early in his career while contractually restricted to working only for his employer, architect Louis Sullivan.
distanceFromLoop: About 13 miles west of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 30-35 minutes via Metra BNSF

name: Hinsdale
vibe: One of the western suburbs' most affluent communities, known for large lots and new construction teardowns.
description: Hinsdale combines a historic downtown with a significant amount of new-construction luxury homes built on teardown lots, and is consistently ranked among the top school districts in Illinois.
commute: Metra BNSF, roughly 30-35 minutes to the Loop
schools: Hinsdale Central High School
photoCredit: Downtown Hinsdale Historic District, photo by Teemu08, CC BY-SA 3.0, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Downtown Hinsdale is a National Register Historic District, largely unchanged despite the intense teardown-and-rebuild activity on surrounding residential blocks.
distanceFromLoop: About 15 miles west of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 35-40 minutes via Metra BNSF

name: Naperville
vibe: One of the largest and most amenity-rich suburbs in the country, with a Riverwalk downtown.
description: Naperville is consistently ranked among the best places to raise a family nationally, with a park district-run Riverwalk, two top-rated high school districts, and enough scale to have its own restaurant and retail ecosystem separate from the city.
commute: Metra BNSF, roughly 40-50 minutes to the Loop
schools: Naperville Central / Naperville North High Schools
famousResidents: Evan Lysacek — Olympic gold medalist figure skater
Candace Parker — WNBA champion and Olympic gold medalist
funFacts: Naperville's Riverwalk, built by volunteers in 1981 for the city's 150th anniversary, is widely credited as the turnaround point for its downtown.
rankings: #1 Best City to Live in America — Niche.com, 2025 and 2026
Best Public Schools in America — Niche.com
No. 16, Best Places to Live — Money Magazine, 2022
distanceFromLoop: About 28 miles west of the Loop — roughly 40-50 minutes by car, 60-70 minutes via Metra BNSF
photoCredit: Naperville Riverwalk, via Wikimedia Commons

name: Downers Grove
vibe: A well-regarded, slightly more affordable alternative to Hinsdale and Naperville.
description: Downers Grove offers strong schools and a walkable downtown at a lower price point than neighboring Hinsdale, drawing families who want the same commuter rail access without the premium.
commute: Metra BNSF, roughly 35-40 minutes to the Loop
schools: Downers Grove North / South High Schools
photoCredit: Downers Grove Metra station, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Bob Bryar — drummer for My Chemical Romance
funFacts: The Tivoli, built in 1928, was the first theater in DuPage County to show "talkies" and still plays its original Wurlitzer theater organ before weekend evening screenings.
distanceFromLoop: About 20 miles west of the Loop — roughly 30-40 minutes by car, 45-55 minutes via Metra BNSF

name: Arlington Heights
vibe: A walkable downtown built around its Metra station, plus the site of the former Arlington Park racetrack now slated for major redevelopment.
description: Arlington Heights has one of the northwest suburbs' most successful transit-oriented downtowns, and is at the center of a major long-term redevelopment plan for the former Arlington International Racecourse site.
commute: Metra Union Pacific Northwest, roughly 40-50 minutes to the Loop
schools: Prospect / Rolling Meadows High Schools
photoCredit: Arlington Heights Historic District, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Was known as 'Dunton,' after original settler William Dunton, until 1874.
Formerly home to The Cellar (1964-1970), a teen rock club that hosted acts including The Who, The Byrds, and Cream.
distanceFromLoop: About 25 miles northwest of the Loop — roughly 40-45 minutes by car, 50-60 minutes via Metra Union Pacific Northwest

name: Schaumburg
vibe: A major retail and corporate hub anchored by Woodfield Mall.
description: Schaumburg is one of the region's largest job and retail centers outside the city, home to Woodfield Mall and a large concentration of corporate offices, making it popular for buyers who work locally rather than commuting downtown.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District West (nearby Schaumburg/Roselle stops), roughly 50-60 minutes to the Loop
schools: Schaumburg / Hoffman Estates High Schools
photoCredit: Schaumburg Metra station, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Susan Downey — film producer, wife of Robert Downey Jr.
funFacts: Woodfield Mall, which opened in 1971, is the largest shopping mall in Illinois and draws more than 27 million visitors a year.
distanceFromLoop: About 28 miles northwest of the Loop — roughly 40-45 minutes by car, 55-65 minutes via Metra Milwaukee District West

name: Park Ridge
vibe: One of the closest suburbs to the city and O'Hare, with a classic Midwestern downtown.
description: Park Ridge borders the city and sits minutes from O'Hare, giving it an unusually short commute for a suburb along with a walkable downtown and strong schools.
commute: Metra Union Pacific Northwest, roughly 30 minutes to the Loop
schools: Maine South High School
photoCredit: Pickwick Theatre, Park Ridge, via Wikimedia Commons
famousResidents: Hillary Rodham Clinton — grew up in Park Ridge, graduated Maine South High School
Harrison Ford — attended high school in Park Ridge
funFacts: The Art Deco Pickwick Theatre, built in 1928 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places since 1975, was the backdrop for the opening credits of Gene Siskel and Roger Ebert's At the Movies in the 1980s.
distanceFromLoop: About 17 miles northwest of the Loop — roughly 30-35 minutes by car, 40-45 minutes via Metra Union Pacific Northwest

name: Elmhurst
vibe: A leafy, close-in suburb with a compact downtown and its own art museum.
description: Elmhurst sits close to O'Hare and the city, with a walkable downtown, the Elmhurst Art Museum, and consistently strong schools, at a lower price point than Hinsdale.
commute: Metra Union Pacific West, roughly 30 minutes to the Loop
schools: York Community High School
photoCredit: Elmhurst Water Tower, Butterfield Park, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: The annual Elmhurst St. Patrick's Day Parade is the third-largest parade of its kind in the Chicago area.
Elmhurst University (organized in 1871 as Elmhurst College) sits on land donated by Thomas Barbour Bryan, sometimes called the "Father of Elmhurst."
distanceFromLoop: About 16 miles west of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 35-40 minutes via Metra Union Pacific West

name: Orland Park
vibe: The southwest suburbs' commercial and residential hub, with newer housing stock than most Chicago suburbs.
description: Orland Park offers relatively new housing stock, a large retail base, and a well-regarded park district, making it one of the most popular destinations for families relocating to the southwest suburbs.
commute: Metra SouthWest Service, roughly 50-60 minutes to the Loop
schools: Carl Sandburg / Victor J. Andrew High Schools
photoCredit: Orland Park Metra station, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Orland Park is sometimes called the "World's Golf Center" — village lore counts over 1,000 golf holes within a 15-mile radius.
distanceFromLoop: About 25 miles southwest of the Loop — roughly 35-40 minutes by car, 50-60 minutes via Metra SouthWest Service

name: Tinley Park / Frankfort
vibe: Two of the fastest-growing southwest suburbs, with a steady supply of newer subdivisions.
description: Tinley Park and Frankfort have seen significant new-construction growth over the past two decades, offering larger homes on larger lots than closer-in suburbs, at some of the best price-per-square-foot in the metro.
commute: Metra Rock Island / SouthWest Service, roughly 55-65 minutes to the Loop
schools: Lincoln-Way / Tinley Park High Schools
photoCredit: Tinley Park Metra station, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Named in 1890 for Samuel Tinley Sr., the first station agent of the local railroad depot; originally founded in 1853 as "Bremen."
BusinessWeek named Tinley Park the Best Place to Raise a Family in America in 2009.
Ironite, a waterproofing additive later used in the construction of the Hoover Dam, was invented and manufactured in Tinley Park.
distanceFromLoop: About 26 miles southwest of the Loop — roughly 40-45 minutes by car, 55-65 minutes via Metra Rock Island District

name: Oak Lawn
vibe: One of the closest southwest suburbs to the city, with an established, affordable housing stock.
description: Oak Lawn offers one of the shorter commutes among southwest suburbs along with a large stock of well-established ranch and split-level homes at accessible price points.
commute: Metra SouthWest Service, roughly 35-40 minutes to the Loop
schools: Oak Lawn Community High School
photoCredit: West 95th Street, downtown Oak Lawn, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Home to Advocate Christ Medical Center, one of Illinois's largest hospitals and a Level I trauma center that treats more emergency patients than any other hospital in the state.
On April 21, 1967, Oak Lawn was struck by one of the deadliest tornadoes in Illinois history, cutting a roughly 16-mile path of destruction through the village.
distanceFromLoop: About 15 miles southwest of the Loop — roughly 25-30 minutes by car, 40-45 minutes via Metra SouthWest Service

name: Homer Glen / Lockport
vibe: Larger lots and a more rural feel at the outer edge of the southwest suburbs, along the historic I&M Canal.
description: Homer Glen and Lockport sit further from the city but offer noticeably larger lots and lower price points, with Lockport's historic downtown along the I&M Canal adding some small-town character.
commute: Metra Heritage Corridor (limited schedule) or car commute, roughly 60+ minutes to the Loop
schools: Lockport Township High School
photoCredit: The historic drainage canal at Lockport, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Lockport was the headquarters of the Illinois and Michigan Canal, and takes its name from the canal's Lock No. 1, still visible downtown.
The Lockport Historic District includes 59 buildings, most built of locally quarried limestone, including the 1838 Gaylord Building, now a restaurant and museum.
distanceFromLoop: About 30 miles southwest of the Loop — roughly 45-55 minutes by car (Lockport); Homer Glen is a few miles further west with no rail service, car-dependent

name: Riverside
vibe: A National Historic Landmark village designed in 1869 by Frederick Law Olmsted, with curving streets following the Des Plaines River.
description: Riverside is one of the first planned suburban communities in the U.S., designed by Frederick Law Olmsted (of Central Park fame) around curving roads and river-following green space rather than a grid. The entire village is a National Historic Landmark.
commute: Metra BNSF Line, roughly 25-30 minutes to Union Station
schools: Riverside-Brookfield High School; Riverside Elementary District 96
photoCredit: Riverside Historic District, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: Designed in 1869 by Frederick Law Olmsted and Calvert Vaux (the team behind Central Park), Riverside is considered the first fully realized planned suburb in the United States.
Frank Lloyd Wright's Avery Coonley House, a National Historic Landmark in its own right, is one of more than 80 landmark structures in the village.
rankings: Named one of the Illinois 200 Great Places by AIA Illinois in 2018

name: North Riverside
vibe: A small, affordable residential village best known regionally for the North Riverside Park Mall.
description: North Riverside is a small, mostly residential village next to Riverside, offering more affordable housing than many of its Near West neighbors along with easy expressway access via I-290.
commute: Pace bus to CTA/Metra connections; car-dependent, roughly 20-25 minutes to the Loop via I-290
schools: Riverside-Brookfield High School; North Riverside School District 96
photoCredit: North Riverside Park Mall, via Wikimedia Commons
funFacts: North Riverside Park Mall, which opened in 1975, had scenes from the 2011 film Contagion filmed there.
The Village Commons, the civic heart of town, features a pond, fountain, and walking paths and is considered one of the more attractive public green spaces in the western suburbs.
distanceFromLoop: About 10 miles west of the Loop — roughly 20-25 minutes by car via I-290

name: Berwyn
vibe: A dense, historic suburb known for its Chicago-bungalow housing stock and the giant fiberglass "Spindle" car sculpture (removed 2008, still part of local lore).
description: Berwyn is a working-class-rooted, densely built suburb known for mile after mile of Chicago bungalows, a walkable downtown, and some of the most affordable housing close to the city in the western suburbs.
commute: Metra BNSF Line, roughly 20-25 minutes to Union Station
schools: Morton West/Freshman Center; multiple elementary districts

name: Western Springs
vibe: A small, family-oriented village known for top-rated schools and a classic downtown Metra stop.
description: Western Springs is a small, affluent, family-focused village with a walkable downtown centered on its Metra station, and consistently strong elementary and high school ratings.
commute: Metra BNSF Line, roughly 25-30 minutes to Union Station
schools: Lyons Township High School; Western Springs School District 101

name: Brookfield
vibe: Best known as home to the Brookfield Zoo, with a mix of bungalows and post-war housing at accessible prices.
description: Brookfield is a family-friendly suburb best known for the Brookfield Zoo (officially the Chicago Zoological Society), offering more affordable housing than some of its Near West neighbors along with Metra access.
commute: Metra BNSF Line, roughly 25-30 minutes to Union Station
schools: Riverside-Brookfield High School; Brookfield LaGrange Park School District 95

name: Des Plaines
vibe: A larger, diverse suburb next to O'Hare, with a walkable downtown Metra stop and generally accessible pricing.
description: Des Plaines is a sizable, diverse suburb bordering O'Hare International Airport, offering a walkable downtown around its Metra station along with generally more accessible pricing than the North Shore.
commute: Metra Union Pacific Northwest Line + CTA Blue Line (Rosemont) nearby, roughly 30-35 minutes to the Loop
schools: Maine West High School; multiple elementary districts

name: Skokie
vibe: One of the most ethnically diverse suburbs in the country, with a direct CTA Yellow Line connection into the city.
description: Skokie is a diverse, well-established suburb directly north of Chicago, known for its strong public schools, the Illinois Holocaust Museum, and being one of the only suburbs with a direct CTA rapid transit connection via the Yellow Line.
commute: CTA Yellow Line (Skokie Swift) to the Red Line, roughly 30-35 minutes to the Loop; Metra UP-North also nearby
schools: Niles North/Niles West High Schools; Skokie School District 69/73.5

name: Buffalo Grove
vibe: A planned, family-oriented suburb straddling Lake and Cook counties, consistently ranked among the safer, higher-scoring school suburbs.
description: Buffalo Grove is a master-planned suburb known for strong schools, extensive park district programming, and a diverse, family-focused population.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District North (nearby Prairie View/Wheeling), roughly 45-55 minutes to Union Station; car-dependent for most
schools: Buffalo Grove/Stevenson High Schools; Kildeer Countryside and other elementary districts

name: Lake Forest
vibe: One of the North Shore's most affluent, historic communities, with large wooded lots and a private lakefront.
description: Lake Forest is among the wealthiest and most historic North Shore suburbs, known for large wooded estate lots, a well-preserved historic downtown (Market Square, one of the first planned shopping centers in the U.S.), and Lake Forest College.
commute: Metra Union Pacific North Line, roughly 45-55 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Lake Forest High School; Lake Forest School District 67

name: Riverwoods
vibe: A small, densely wooded, large-lot village with no traditional downtown — privacy is the draw.
description: Riverwoods is a small, exclusive village built around large wooded lots and winding, wooded roads rather than a traditional commercial downtown, prized for privacy and space.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra is Deerfield (UP-North), roughly 45-55 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Deerfield High School; Riverwoods School District 96

name: Libertyville
vibe: A charming, historic downtown suburb with strong schools and an active Metra commuter base.
description: Libertyville pairs a walkable, historic downtown with strong schools and larger lot sizes than closer-in suburbs, drawing families who want small-town character on the Metra line.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District North, roughly 55-65 minutes to Union Station
schools: Libertyville High School; Libertyville School District 70

name: Deerfield
vibe: A high-performing-school North Shore suburb also home to major corporate campuses (Walgreens, Beam Suntory, Mondelez).
description: Deerfield combines top-ranked schools with a significant corporate presence, drawing families who want North Shore school quality with a shorter commute for some via nearby employers.
commute: Metra Union Pacific North Line, roughly 45-50 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Deerfield High School; Deerfield School District 109

name: Lincolnshire
vibe: A smaller, golf-course-lined village home to the Marriott Lincolnshire Resort and several corporate offices.
description: Lincolnshire is a smaller North Shore-area village built around golf courses and corporate campuses, with a quieter, lower-density residential feel than its larger neighbors.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra in Deerfield/Prairie View, roughly 50-60 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Adlai E. Stevenson High School; Aptakisic-Tripp School District 102

name: Kildeer
vibe: A small, upscale village known for large-lot estates and Kemper Lakes Golf Club.
description: Kildeer is a small, affluent village near Long Grove known for large-lot custom homes and its golf-course setting, feeding into the highly regarded Stevenson High School.
commute: Car-dependent; roughly 55-65 minutes to the Loop by car depending on traffic
schools: Adlai E. Stevenson High School; Kildeer Countryside School District 96

name: Northbrook
vibe: A well-established North Shore suburb known for Glenbrook North High School and easy expressway/Metra access.
description: Northbrook is a family-oriented North Shore suburb with strong schools, a walkable downtown, and convenient access to both the Edens Expressway and Metra.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District North, roughly 40-45 minutes to Union Station
schools: Glenbrook North High School; Northbrook School District 27/28/30

name: Palos Park
vibe: A wooded, semi-rural village bordering the Palos Forest Preserves, popular with buyers who want space and privacy.
description: Palos Park is a small, heavily wooded village bordering the extensive Palos Forest Preserves, drawing buyers who want larger, private lots and proximity to hiking and nature trails while staying in the southwest suburbs.
commute: Metra SouthWest Service, roughly 45-55 minutes to Union Station
schools: Amos Alonzo Stagg High School; Palos Community Consolidated School District 118

name: Palos Heights
vibe: A quiet, lake-and-park-centered village anchored by Lake Katherine Nature Center.
description: Palos Heights is a quiet, residential southwest suburb centered on Lake Katherine Nature Preserve, with a family-oriented feel and easy access to the Palos forest preserve system.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra in Palos Park/Worth, roughly 45-55 minutes to Union Station
schools: Amos Alonzo Stagg / Chicago Christian High Schools; multiple elementary districts

name: Burr Ridge
vibe: An upscale village known for larger estate homes, golf clubs, and easy access to I-55, I-294, and I-355.
description: Burr Ridge is an affluent village known for larger custom and estate homes, several golf and country clubs, and unusually convenient access to three major expressways.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra in Hinsdale/Willowbrook, roughly 35-40 minutes to Union Station
schools: Hinsdale Central High School; Pleasantdale/Gower School Districts

name: Bolingbrook
vibe: One of the larger, fast-growing southwest suburbs, known for relatively affordable newer construction.
description: Bolingbrook is one of the larger southwest suburbs, having grown rapidly since the 1980s, offering a wide range of newer-construction housing at prices generally more accessible than closer-in suburbs.
commute: Car-dependent (no direct Metra); Pace bus connections, roughly 40-50 minutes to the Loop by car
schools: Bolingbrook High School; Valley View School District 365U

name: Lemont
vibe: A historic former quarry town along the Des Plaines River and I&M Canal, with a walkable, limestone-built downtown.
description: Lemont's downtown is built largely of locally quarried limestone, a legacy of its 19th-century quarrying industry along the Illinois and Michigan Canal, giving it one of the more distinctive historic Main Streets in the southwest suburbs.
commute: Metra Heritage Corridor, limited weekday service, roughly 45-50 minutes to Union Station
schools: Lemont High School; Lemont-Bromberek School District 113A

name: Crystal Lake
vibe: A larger McHenry County suburb built around its namesake spring-fed lake, offering more space for the price than closer-in suburbs.
description: Crystal Lake is a larger, farther-out suburb centered on its namesake spring-fed lake, offering more house and land for the money than suburbs closer to the city, at the cost of a longer commute.
commute: Metra Union Pacific Northwest Line, roughly 65-75 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Crystal Lake Central/South High Schools; multiple elementary districts

name: Glen Ellyn
vibe: A walkable, tree-lined DuPage County suburb with a well-preserved downtown and strong schools.
description: Glen Ellyn is a walkable, well-established DuPage County suburb known for its historic downtown, Lake Ellyn park, and consistently strong-performing schools.
commute: Metra Union Pacific West Line, roughly 40-45 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Glenbard West High School; Glen Ellyn School District 41

name: Carol Stream
vibe: A larger, mostly residential DuPage County suburb offering more accessible pricing than its immediate neighbors.
description: Carol Stream is a mostly residential DuPage County suburb, developed largely in the postwar era, offering more accessible housing prices than closer-in DuPage suburbs like Glen Ellyn or Wheaton.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra in Wheaton/Winfield, roughly 45-55 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Glenbard North/Wheaton North High Schools; Community Consolidated School District 93

name: St. Charles
vibe: A picturesque Fox River town with a historic downtown, popular for its riverfront setting and small-town character.
description: St. Charles sits directly on the Fox River, with a historic, walkable downtown of 19th-century limestone buildings and a strong small-town identity distinct from closer-in Chicago suburbs.
commute: Car-dependent (no Metra in St. Charles proper; nearest is Geneva), roughly 60-70 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: St. Charles East/North High Schools; St. Charles School District 303

name: Batavia
vibe: A historic Fox River mill town, known as the "Windmill City," with a walkable downtown and accessible pricing relative to neighboring Fox Valley towns.
description: Batavia grew up around Fox River mills and windmill manufacturing in the 19th century, and today offers a walkable historic downtown with generally more accessible pricing than nearby Geneva or St. Charles.
commute: Metra Union Pacific West Line, roughly 65-75 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Batavia High School; Batavia Public School District 101

name: Aurora
vibe: Illinois's second-largest city, spanning the Fox River, offering the widest range of price points of any suburb on this list.
description: Aurora is Illinois's second-largest city by population, a diverse, sprawling community along the Fox River with a historic downtown (including the restored Paramount Theatre) and the widest range of housing prices and styles of any community in this guide.
commute: Metra BNSF Line (express service available), roughly 60-70 minutes to Union Station
schools: Multiple high schools across several districts (East, West, Indian Prairie, Oswego)

name: Barrington
vibe: A cluster of Barrington-area communities known for large-lot, semi-rural estates and equestrian culture.
description: The Barrington area (Barrington, North Barrington, South Barrington, Barrington Hills) is known for large-lot, semi-rural estates, horse farms, and some of the most land-per-dollar of any suburb within reasonable commuting distance of Chicago.
commute: Metra Union Pacific Northwest Line, roughly 60-70 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Barrington High School; Barrington Community Unit School District 220

name: Lake Zurich
vibe: A lake-centered Lake County suburb with a walkable, revitalized downtown near the water.
description: Lake Zurich is built around its namesake lake, with a downtown that has seen significant revitalization in recent years, drawing families who want lake access without North Shore prices.
commute: Car-dependent; nearest Metra in Barrington/Long Grove area, roughly 60-70 minutes to Ogilvie
schools: Lake Zurich High School; Lake Zurich Community Unit School District 95

name: Vernon Hills
vibe: A newer, planned Lake County suburb known for major shopping centers and family-friendly subdivisions.
description: Vernon Hills is a newer, largely master-planned Lake County suburb, known regionally for major retail (Hawthorn Mall) alongside family-oriented subdivisions and good access to the Tri-State Tollway.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District North (nearby stations), roughly 55-65 minutes to Union Station
schools: Vernon Hills / Libertyville High Schools; Fremont/Hawthorn School Districts

name: Niles
vibe: A close-in, diverse Northwest suburb known for its own leaning Tower of Pisa replica and accessible pricing.
description: Niles is a close-in, diverse Northwest suburb offering generally accessible pricing and quick access to the city, with a well-known local landmark: a half-scale replica of the Leaning Tower of Pisa.
commute: CTA Blue/Yellow Line connections nearby; car/bus-dependent, roughly 30-35 minutes to the Loop
schools: Maine East/Notre Dame High Schools; Niles Elementary School District 71

name: Morton Grove
vibe: A quiet, close-in Northwest suburb with a mix of postwar housing and easy access to the Edens Expressway.
description: Morton Grove is a quiet, family-oriented Northwest suburb with a largely postwar housing stock, offering solid value and convenient access to both the Edens Expressway and Metra.
commute: Metra Milwaukee District North, roughly 35-40 minutes to Union Station
schools: Niles West High School; Morton Grove School District 70
